35: arxamenos apo tes graphes tautes euengelisato auto ton Iesoun (arxamenos apo tēs graphēs tautēs euēngelisato autō ton Iēsoun): 'beginning from this Scripture he proclaimed good news about Jesus'. Philip's method is the apostolic hermeneutic: Isa 53 → Jesus. euangelizomai here has its full weight as announcement of a decisive new development — the Servant's identity is now disclosed. The Ethiopian eunuch's question ('of himself or another?') echoes the pesher method of Qumran: Scripture requires an identified referent to unlock its meaning.
1: syneudokōn (syneudokōn, 'consenting/approving'): Paul will later describe his own role at Stephen's death with the same verb (22:20) — he was not a passive bystander but an active approver. diōgmos megas (diōgmos megas, 'great persecution'): the first use of diōgmos in Acts; the persecution Paul later says he suffered becomes the persecution he first caused (Gal 1:13; 1 Cor 15:9).
2: andres eulabeis (andres eulabeis, 'devout men'): the same adjective used for Simeon (Luke 2:25) and Cornelius (Acts 10:2). These are Torah-observant Jews honoring a fellow-Jew whom they recognized as righteous; their mourning (penthountes) was public and costly — lamentation for one executed as a criminal was itself a social risk.
3: elymaineto (elymaineto, 'was ravaging/destroying'): a verb used in LXX for wild animals tearing flesh (Ps 80:13); its application to Saul's house-to-house arrests is deliberately brutal. syron (syrōn, 'dragging'): the violent participle conveys forced arrest — men and women dragged from their homes and thrown into prison. This is Paul's own retrospective self-condemnation (1 Tim 1:13: 'the chief of sinners').
4: hoi men oun diasparentes (hoi men oun diasparentes, 'those who were scattered'): diaspeirō (scatter, disperse) is the diaspora verb; the persecution that intended to suppress the gospel instead geographically expands it. Luke's narrative irony is deliberate: the enemy's strategy becomes the mission's vehicle. euangelizomenoi (proclaiming good news): the scattered are not refugees in silence but active evangelists.
5: Philippos ... ekērysso autois ton Christon (Philippos ... ekērysso autois ton Christon, 'Philip proclaimed the Messiah to them'): Philip is the deacon (6:5) whose mission reaches Samaria — the next step in Acts 1:8's geographic outline. Kēryssō (herald-proclamation) is the strong word for authoritative announcement; its object is the Messiah-title, the full Jewish category that requires hermeneutical explanation.
6: homothymadon (with one accord): the same word as Pentecost (2:1, 46), now describing the Samaritan crowd's unified attention. sēmeia ha epoiei (signs he was doing): the signs are the confirmation that the proclamation is authentic — same pattern as Peter and Paul. The Samaritans, traditionally estranged from Jerusalem, respond with the same unity as the Jerusalem crowd.
7: pneumata akatharta ... exērchetho (impure spirits were coming out): akatharta (unclean) is the ritual-purity vocabulary applied to the demonic realm — the mission in Samaria addresses both spiritual and physical disorder. parelymenoi kai chōloi etherapeuonto (paralyzed and lame were healed): the two categories appear in the Isa 35:6 messianic-sign list, echoing Peter's temple healing.
8: egeneto chara megalē (egeneto chara megalē, 'there was great joy'): joy is Luke's theological signal for eschatological arrival (Luke 2:10; 24:52; Acts 2:46; 13:52; 15:3; 16:34). The Samaritan city's joy marks the mission's reach into the traditionally excluded Samaritans; the kingdom's arrival is experienced as joy wherever it goes.
9: mageuōn (mageuōn, 'practicing sorcery/magic'): the root gives us 'magic'; Simon is not merely a conjurer but someone operating within the framework of Hellenistic magical practice — invoking divine names and powers for results. existanōn (amazing): the same verb Philip's signs produce (v.6); Simon's amazement-capacity is about to be displaced by a greater power.
10: hē dynamis tou theou hē kaloumenē Megalē (hē dynamis tou theou hē kaloumenē Megalē, 'the power of God called Great'): Simon's title is a Samaritan divine-name, likely connected to Simonian Gnosticism's later 'Great Power' theology. Luke preserves the exact wording — a measure of accuracy about a religious milieu his audience would recognize. Philip's proclamation of Jesus as Lord displaces Simon's self-claimed divine-power identity.
11: dia to hikanō chronō tais mageiais existakenai autous ('because for a long time he had amazed them with his sorceries'): hikanos chronos (a long/considerable time) measures the depth of Simon's hold on the community; the Samaritans are not casually credulous but have been formed by years of his practice. Philip's sudden displacement of Simon signals the superior power of the gospel's witness.
12: euangelizomenō peri tēs basileias tou theou kai tou onomatos Iēsou Christou ('proclaiming the good news about the kingdom of God and the name of Jesus Christ'): the two objects — kingdom and name — are the content of the Samaritan proclamation. Both men and women (andres te kai gynaikes) are baptized; the mission to Samaria includes women from the beginning, consistent with Acts' consistent inclusion of women in the covenant community.
13: ho de Simōn kai autos episteusen ('Simon himself also believed'): Luke records Simon's belief and baptism factually; the subsequent exchange (vv.18-24) does not retract the belief but reveals its inadequate root. theōrōn ... dynameis kai sēmeia ginomena ('seeing the signs and great miracles happening'): Simon's continued astonishment is not penitential but acquisitive — he watches as a professional assessor of power.
14: aposteilan pros autous Petron kai Iōannēn (sent Peter and John): Jerusalem's apostolic response to Samaritan conversion establishes continuity with the mother-community; the Samaritans are not a breakaway sect but an extension of the one church. The Jerusalem apostles' visit is the structural incorporation of Samaria into the body that began at Pentecost.
15: hopōs labōsin pneuma hagion (so that they might receive the Holy Spirit): the separation between belief/baptism and Spirit-reception here is the strongest in Acts; it has generated debate about whether this was a temporary transitional phenomenon (incorporating Samaria required apostolic laying on of hands) or a normative two-stage pattern. The delay is not explained; Luke presents the apostolic prayer and hands as the channel for the Spirit's arrival.
16: oupō gar ēn ep' oudeni autōn epipeptōkos ('for he had not yet fallen on any of them'): epipiptō (fall upon) is the Pentecost-verb (10:44; 11:15); its conspicuous absence here makes the contrast pointed. The Spirit who fell on 120 in Jerusalem without laying on of hands had not fallen on the Samaritan believers who had received baptism — the anomaly drives the narrative forward.
17: epetithoun tas cheiras (they were laying their hands on them): the apostolic laying on of hands as the instrument for Spirit-reception appears here for the first time in Acts. The gesture has OT precedent (Num 8:10; 27:18-23) for commissioning and authorization; its use here extends that tradition to Spirit-reception, making the apostles' bodily action the channel of the divine gift.
18: theōn hoti dia tēs epithesesōs tōn cheirōn tōn apostolōn didotai to Pneuma ('seeing that through the laying on of the apostles' hands the Spirit was given'): Simon sees something visible — the Spirit's reception was evidentially manifest, likely through speaking in tongues. His response reveals that he has processed Philip's entire ministry as a power-system with a controlling mechanism he could learn to operate.
19: dos kai emoi tēn exousian tautēn ('give me also this authority'): exousia (authority, right) is precisely what Simon thinks he wants — the ability to transmit the Spirit by personal action. He misidentifies the Spirit's gift as a power that belongs to the apostles and can be purchased from them. The sin of simony names a category of ecclesiastical corruption after this episode.
20: to argyrion sou syn soi eiē eis apōleian ('may your money go to destruction with you'): Peter's response is the sharpest rebuke in Acts — not a question but a wish-formula invoking divine judgment. hoti tēn dōrean tou theou enomisas dia chrēmatōn ktasthai ('because you thought you could acquire the gift of God with money'): dōrea (gift, v.20) echoes 2:38; the Spirit is given, never purchased.
21: ouk estin soi meris oud' klēros en tō logō toutō ('you have no part or share in this word'): meris and klēros are the inheritance-vocabulary of covenant participation — the same terms used for Judas's forfeited portion (1:17, 25). Simon's heart (hē kardia sou ouk estin eutheia enanti tou theou) is the diagnostic: not the money but the disposition of heart is what disqualifies.
22: metanōeson apo tēs kakias tautes ('repent of this wickedness'): Peter's imperative is conditional but genuine — repentance is possible and offered. ei ara aphethēsetai soi hē epinoia tēs kardias sou ('if perhaps the thought of your heart may be forgiven'): the ei ara (if perhaps) is an unusual conditional that indicates uncertainty about whether the intent is forgivable — not denying God's forgiveness but questioning Simon's sincerity.
23: cholēn pikrias kai syndesmō adikias ('gall of bitterness and bond of wickedness'): cholē pikria (gall of bitterness) echoes Deut 29:18 LXX for the root of apostasy — a root of poison-plant. Syndesmos adikias (bond of unrighteousness) describes being bound up in sin's system. Peter's diagnosis reaches back to OT prophetic categories for describing the state of someone whose orientation is fundamentally against the covenant.
24: deēthēte hymeis hyper emou pros ton kyrion ('pray to the Lord for me'): Simon's request — asking others to pray for him rather than praying himself — may indicate genuine fear without genuine repentance. The ambiguity is deliberate; Luke does not close the question of Simon's ultimate state. The narrative serves as a warning rather than a resolution.
25: hypestraphon eis Ierousalēm ... euēngelizonto pollas kōmas tōn Samaritōn ('they returned to Jerusalem, proclaiming good news to many Samaritan villages'): the apostles' return journey through Samaritan villages is a quiet expansion of the mission. The same apostles sent from Jerusalem to evaluate the Samaritan mission become missionaries in Samaritan villages on their way home — the mission spreads through every movement.
26: angelos de kyriou elalēsen pros Philippon (an angel of the Lord spoke to Philip): divine direction by angel (also 5:19; 10:3; 12:7-11; 27:23) is Luke's way of marking pivotal mission-transitions. The road to Gaza is described as autē estin erēmos ('this is a desert [road]') — either the road is remote or Gaza itself was ruined after its destruction in 96 BC. Philip is redirected from successful urban mission to an isolated road encounter.
27: dynastēs Kandakēs basylissēs Aithiopōn ('an official of Candace, queen of the Ethiopians'): dynastēs (high official, powerful person) is a court title; Candace was the title of the queen mother in Meroitic Nubia, who effectively ruled. Ethiopia (Aithiopia) represented the southern end of the known world; this official is both the 'ends of the earth' (Acts 1:8) and a diaspora worshipper connecting the mission to its furthest geographic range.
28: anaginōskōn ton prophētēn Esaian (reading the prophet Isaiah): ancient reading was typically oral — anaginōskōn implies reading aloud, which is why Philip could hear what the eunuch was reading from a distance. The Isaiah scroll was among the most expensive texts in the first century; this official was both literate and wealthy enough to own the text. Luke's detail explains the mechanics of the encounter.
29: eipen de to Pneuma tō Philippō (the Spirit said to Philip): the Spirit who directed Philip to leave Samaria now specifically directs him to approach the chariot. The mission is driven entirely by divine initiative — Philip did not select this target, assess the eunuch's readiness, or choose the moment. Luke uses 'Spirit said' (as opposed to 'angel said' in v.26) to mark the direct Spirit-prompting of the apostolic word.
30: ara ge ginōskeis ha anaginōskeis (ara ge ginōskeis ha anaginōskeis, 'do you understand what you are reading?'): the ara ge formula is an inferential particle combination suggesting 'I take it then' — Philip's question is not impolite but engaged. The paronomasia (the same root ginōskō/anaginōskō) puns on knowing and reading: understanding requires more than literacy.
31: pōs gar an dynaimen ean mē tis hodēgēsei me ('how can I, unless someone guides me?'): hodēgēsē (guide, lead the way) is the road-guide verb; the metaphor fits the chariot journey. The eunuch's honest acknowledgment of interpretive need is exactly the posture that opens him to Philip's instruction. He invites Philip up — the physical proximity enables the hermeneutical conversation.
32: periochē tēs graphēs hēn anaginōsken ('the passage of Scripture he was reading'): periochē (section, passage) is a technical term for a scriptural unit. Isa 53:7-8 LXX is quoted verbatim; the LXX's tēn genean autou tis diēgēsetai diverges from the MT but makes the same point about the Servant's obscure destiny. The sheep-to-slaughter image and the silence of the shearee make Isa 53 the perfect locus for the eunuch's question.
33: en tē tapeinōsei autou hē krisis autou ērthē ('in his humiliation his judgment was taken away'): the LXX reads differently from MT at this point; the Greek version the eunuch was reading has 'in his humiliation his judgment was removed' — his justice was denied, not that he was condemned. The verse supports the proclamation of the innocent sufferer. tis diēgēsetai tēn genean autou: 'who can tell of his descendants' — his life-line cut off, yet the community of believers is precisely that continuation.
34: peri tinos ho prophētēs legei touto; peri heautou ē peri heterou tinos ('about whom does the prophet say this, about himself or about someone else?'): the eunuch's question is the decisive hermeneutical query — the same question the Qumran community asked of Isaiah. Philip's answer begins 'from this Scripture' (apo tēs graphēs tautes, v.35) and proceeds to Jesus: the prophet spoke of another, and that other is Jesus.
36: idou hydōr; ti kōlyei me baptisthēnai ('look, water! what prevents me from being baptized?'): kōlyō (prevent, hinder) is Acts' baptism-readiness question; it reappears in 10:47 (Peter: 'can anyone withhold water?') and 11:17. The eunuch's spontaneous recognition that Philip's proclamation leads to baptism shows genuine understanding — he has connected the Isaiah Servant with Jesus with himself and with the covenant rite.
37: Verse 37 (Philip's 'if you believe with all your heart' exchange) is absent from the earliest and best manuscripts (P45, P74, Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) and is likely a scribal addition providing a baptismal confession formula for liturgical use. Its inclusion in later manuscripts reflects the growing importance of explicit pre-baptismal confession in church practice. The MKT notes its presence because some traditions rely on it.
38: katebēsan amphoteroi eis to hydōr ('both went down into the water'): the immersion implied by eis to hydōr (into the water) and the parallel rising (anebēsan ek tou hydatos, v.39) supports baptism by immersion as the early form. The Sahidic Coptic and Ethiopian liturgical traditions made this the definitive text for immersion-practice; Philip's gesture of accompanying the eunuch into the water marks pastoral solidarity.
39: pneuma kyriou hērpasen ton Philippon ('the Spirit of the Lord suddenly seized/snatched Philip'): harpazō (snatch, catch away) is the violent displacement verb — the same used for Paul's third heaven vision (2 Cor 12:2-4) and the Elijah/Elisha translation tradition (2 Kgs 2:16). Philip is providentially removed; the eunuch 'went on his way rejoicing' (charōn) — the joy-marker of arrival at salvation again.
40: Azōton (Azotus/Ashdod): the Philistine city on the coastal plain; Philip's post-harpazō ministry moves northward through all the coastal towns until Caesarea, where he will appear again as the host of Paul (21:8). The coastal plain mission foreshadows the Gentile mission to come; Philip's itinerary traces a line that will become the spine of the Western Gentile expansion.
